Basically, we have in Python, of course, there are the Pydantic models that do the validation, but there is a second layer. on top of it, because it is all stored in as a JSON document in Postgres. It is not made a relational database that would define all the relations, but instead is, let's say we kept flexibility, although not really, of having just one column we have huge. JSON document. And then we are using a JSON schema that every time we insert this JSON document into the database, it is validated against the JSON schema. And now the problem is that every time we would think, okay, maybe we don't want this kind of threshold, but a little bit different. Now we have to change all the models and then we have to generate the new JSON schema, insert the new JSON schema in Postgres for the new possibility to work. **(00:35:35) Haaß, Martin | tomorrowthings**

**(00:35:36) Riester, Rafael | tomorrowthings**
Yes.

**(00:35:37) Haaß, Martin | tomorrowthings**
because you don't have any fast lookup in a file system and JIT in the end is a file system, needs a file system.

**(00:35:42) Riester, Rafael | tomorrowthings**
Okay.

**(00:35:48) Haaß, Martin | tomorrowthings**
And versioning, you heard versioning and you went to Git, but versioning has a different meaning here as well. It's not enough to... have different versions of the schema. you need mechanics to go from one version to the other because old machines that have never been updated will still be in the old format. And either you keep the old logic around

**(00:36:14) Riester, Rafael | tomorrowthings**
Mhm. Mhm. Yeah.

**(00:36:23) Haaß, Martin | tomorrowthings**
or whenever the old machine is being loaded from the storage, whatever database or file system, it doesn't matter. Whenever some old thing is loaded, it needs to be forward converted to the current expected schema, otherwise the business logic will fail.

**(00:36:41) Riester, Rafael | tomorrowthings**
Mhm.

**(00:36:44) Haaß, Martin | tomorrowthings**
So either multiple business logic versions for each schema version or a conversion from old schema versions to modern. version and only one set of business logic, or continuous data migration each time the schema version is changed, pull all the entries from the database, forward convert them, write them back to the database, and ensure you only have the newest version.

**(00:37:11) Riester, Rafael | tomorrowthings**
Mhm. Yeah. Mhm.

**(00:37:18) Haaß, Martin | tomorrowthings**
That's the three logical only possibilities.
